YUAN-CIZ-PIL-LIO-018  ·  ro-yuan-018

Yuan Cizhou Ceramic Headrest with Lion and Peony Decoration

元代磁州窑狮子牡丹纹瓷枕 — Yuándài Cízhōu Yáo Shīzi Mǔdān Wén Cí Zhěn

Cizhou Ware 磁州窑 Yuan Dynasty · 1279–1368 CE North China Cizhou Tradition Ceramic Headrest · Zhěn 枕 Sgraffito · Iron-Brown Pigment Dronova Method Applied

Object Description

This ceramic headrest (zhěn, 枕) represents a technically accomplished and iconographically complete example of Yuan dynasty Cizhou-type stoneware produced within the north Chinese kiln tradition. The vessel presents the canonical rectangular block form with a gently concave upper resting surface, softly rounded corners, slightly tapering walls, hollow interior construction, and a stable rectangular base — proportions closely paralleled by authenticated Yuan ceramic pillows preserved in the Palace Museum (Beijing), Shanghai Museum, Nelson-Atkins Museum of Art, Victoria and Albert Museum, and Asian Art Museum (San Francisco).

The decorative programme is executed in the iron-brown-on-white-slip technique characteristic of the Cizhou tradition: a buff-grey high-fired stoneware body receives an even white slip layer, over which iron-rich mineral pigment is freely painted in bold brushwork. The composition is further elaborated through sgraffito — incision through the wet pigment layer to expose the cream slip beneath, creating fine linear details of the lion's mane, lotus pods, and peony petals that contrast with the deep brown paintwork.

The principal motif is a guardian lion (shīzi, 狮子) shown in dynamic movement with a lion cub, surrounded by lotus scrolls and peony blooms. This iconographic programme draws on established Yuan Buddhist and auspicious symbolism — the lion as protector, the peony as emblem of prosperity and nobility, the lotus as symbol of purity and spiritual attainment — forming a coherent programme of protection and good fortune appropriate to the intimate domestic function of the headrest.

Construction: The headrest was formed from a high-fired buff-grey stoneware body with a lateral ventilation aperture — a characteristic feature of authentic kiln manufacture that allowed gases to escape during firing and prevented explosive failure of the hollow form. The interior profile of this aperture shows a rounded, continuous firing surface identical to the exterior body, confirming authentic kiln production rather than later drilling.

Microscopic Analysis

Оптическое исследование проводилось при увеличениях ×10, ×20 и ×40 в режимах отражённого и косого освещения по Методу Дроновой. Исследование шести диагностических зон: морфология, белый ангоб, железный пигмент, техника сграффито, структура глазури и характер износа поверхности.

Zone A — Сграффито, грива льва

Макро · ×10
×10 Macro

Сграффито выполнено прорезкой сырого пигмента до кремового ангоба. Нерегулярные края разреза, переменная глубина прорезки, естественные прерывания линий. Признаков механической гравировки нет. Кистевая работа сохраняет естественную вариацию нажима.

Грива льва проработана техникой сграффито: острым инструментом прорезан влажный слой железного пигмента, обнажая кремовый ангоб. Линии варьируют по ширине и глубине — характерный признак ручного исполнения, не воспроизводимый механически. Параллельные штрихи тела льва и перьеобразные мазки грудной шерсти выполнены уверенными движениями, характерными для опытного юаньского мастера цзычжоуской традиции.

Zone B — Пигмент и ангоб, ×20

Оптический · ×20
×20

Диффузное проникновение железного пигмента в ангоб — признак нанесения на влажную основу. Зернистая текстура белого ангоба соответствует традиции Цзычжоу. Прозрачная глазурь с естественным скоплением в углублениях. Кракле равномерное, без следов искусственного окрашивания.

При ×20 отчётливо видна диффузная граница между железным пигментом и белым ангобом — результат нанесения пигмента по ещё влажному ангобу. Это поведение невозможно воспроизвести при нанесении пигмента на уже обожжённую поверхность и является надёжным диагностическим признаком аутентичной цзычжоуской технологии.

Diagnostic Interpretation

Объект оценивался по одиннадцатикритериальной матрице мультидоказательного протокола аутентификации Метода Дроновой для юаньских изделий Цзычжоу. Все критерии удовлетворены.

Diagnostic Criterion Observed Assessment
Overall morphology & typologyCanonical Yuan rectangular block form; concave upper surface, rounded corners, hollow interior, ventilation hole✓ Strong Positive
Ceramic bodyBuff-grey high-fired stoneware; compact dense fabric; natural mineral inclusions; no modern porosity✓ Strong Positive
White slipEven application; complete coverage; natural ageing; slight translucency; microscopic shrinkage features✓ Strong Positive
Iron-brown pigmentIron-rich mineral pigment; variable brush loading; diffuse penetration into slip; no synthetic particles✓ Strong Positive
Sgraffito techniqueIncision through wet pigment; irregular handmade cutting; variable depth; natural line interruptions✓ Strong Positive
Glaze structureNatural thickness variation; pooling in recesses; numerous microscopic bubbles; early vitrification texture✓ Positive
CraquelureIrregular polygonal geometry; naturally intersecting network; variable crack width; weathered edges✓ Strong Positive
Surface wearEdge abrasion; softened high points; natural handling polish; gradual pigment wear✓ Strong Positive
Ventilation apertureRounded interior profile; continuous firing surface; identical body inside and outside; no secondary drilling✓ Strong Positive
IconographyGuardian lion + lion cub + lotus scrolls + peony — established Yuan Buddhist & auspicious programme✓ Consistent
Museum comparandaClose correspondence with Palace Museum, Shanghai Museum, Nelson-Atkins, V&A, Asian Art Museum examples✓ Strong Positive

Diagnostic Checklist

  • Canonical Yuan meiping rectangular block form with concave rest surface and hollow construction — strong typological indicator
  • Buff-grey high-fired stoneware body with compact dense fabric and natural mineral inclusions consistent with northern Cizhou tradition
  • White slip with even application, natural ageing, and microscopic shrinkage features — no modern overpainting detected
  • Iron-rich mineral pigment with diffuse penetration into slip: confirms application to wet slip, not fired surface
  • Sgraffito: irregular handmade incision through wet pigment with variable depth and natural line interruptions — mechanically impossible to reproduce
  • Transparent glaze with natural thickness variation, pooling in recesses, and microscopic bubble population consistent with authentic high-temperature firing
  • Craquelure: irregular polygonal network with weathered crack edges — centuries of natural ageing, no artificial staining
  • Surface wear follows expected handling patterns: edge abrasion, softened high points, natural polish — differs from artificial abrasion
  • Ventilation aperture with rounded interior profile and continuous firing surface — authentic kiln manufacture confirmed
  • No evidence of modern restoration, repainting, reglaze, artificial ageing, or reproduction characteristics

Diagnostic Fingerprint

Authentication at a glance — полный диагностический портрет объекта.

Diagnostic MarkerAuthentic Yuan Cizhou Example
BodyBuff-grey high-fired stoneware
SlipEven white slip, natural ageing
PigmentIron-brown, diffuse penetration
SgraffitoHand-incised, variable depth
Diagnostic MarkerAuthentic Yuan Cizhou Example
GlazeTransparent, natural bubbles
CraquelureIrregular polygonal, weathered
Vent holeRounded, continuous firing surface
IconographyLion + peony, Yuan auspicious
Совокупность диффузного проникновения пигмента в ангоб, нерегулярного ручного сграффито и выветренного кракле образует диагностическую триаду, которая не воспроизводится при современном искусственном состаривании или механическом производстве. Все три критерия удовлетворяются в данном образце.

Cross-References

ro-yuan-017 — Yuan Black-Glazed Meiping with Dragon

Both objects represent Yuan dynasty stoneware from the southern/northern kiln traditions. Contrast between reserve-incised black glaze (ro-yuan-017) and iron-brown-on-slip sgraffito (ro-yuan-018) illustrates the range of Yuan decorative techniques.

→ View ro-yuan-017

ro-song-008 — Cizhou-Type Painted Pillow

The Cizhou iron-painting tradition spans Song and Yuan periods. Comparison of Song and Yuan Cizhou sgraffito technique and brushwork conventions illustrates the continuity and evolution of the northern stoneware tradition.

→ View ro-song-008

ro-tang-001 — Tang Sancai Camel

Hollow construction comparison: both the Tang sancai camel and this Yuan headrest employ hollow-body construction with ventilation apertures as an integral feature of kiln manufacture.

→ View ro-tang-001
